column1: What is the primary role of a programming language in relation to a computer?
column2: It acts as a tool for programmers to communicate instructions to the machine.

column1: What is the only language that a computer's hardware can natively understand?
column2: Binary language (format of zeros and ones).

column1: Which specific software component is responsible for converting C++ source code into a machine-understandable format?
column2: The Compiler.

column1: What is the common file extension for an executable file in Windows generated after the compilation process?
column2: The .exe extension.

column1: List one high-level benefit of using C++ for system development mentioned in the tutorial.
column2: It provides high performance and efficient execution speed.

column1: In the C++ compilation pipeline, what is the purpose of the 'Linking' stage?
column2: It combines various object files and libraries into a single executable file.

column1: Which C++ header file must be included to utilize standard input and output streams like cin and cout?
column2: $\text{#include <iostream>}$

column1: What does the code $\text{using namespace std;}$ allow a programmer to do?
column2: It allows the use of standard library identifiers without the $\text{std::}$ prefix.

column1: What is considered the mandatory starting point of execution for every C++ program?
column2: The $\text{int main()}$ function.

column1: In C++, what do curly braces $\text{\{ \}}$ typically define within the code structure?
column2: The scope or boundary of a block of code.

column1: What does a $\text{return 0;}$ statement at the end of the $\text{main}$ function signify to the Operating System?
column2: The program has executed successfully without errors.

column1: If a program returns a non-zero value (e.g., $\text{return -1;}$), what does this typically indicate?
column2: The program encountered an error or failed during execution.

column1: In C++, which keyword is used to display or print output to the console window?
column2: $\text{cout}$

column1: What is the purpose of the $\text{endl}$ manipulator in an output statement?
column2: It inserts a newline character and flushes the stream.

column1: What symbol is used in C++ to mark the termination of a statement?
column2: The semicolon ( ; ).

column1: Term: Variable
column2: Definition: A named storage location in memory used to hold data during program execution.

column1: Which C++ data type is specifically used for storing whole numbers (integers)?
column2: $\text{int}$

column1: What is the standard memory size (in bytes) occupied by a $\text{char}$ data type in C++?
column2: 1 byte.

column1: Which data type would you use to store a single letter or symbol, such as 'A' or '$'?
column2: $\text{char}$

column1: How are character literals represented in C++ code to distinguish them from variable names?
column2: They are enclosed in single quotes (e.g., $\text{'a'}$).

column1: What is the typical memory size of a $\text{float}$ data type in modern C++ systems?
column2: 4 bytes.

column1: Which data type is preferred for storing decimal numbers with higher precision and occupies 8 bytes of memory?
column2: $\text{double}$

column1: The $\text{bool}$ data type in C++ can only hold which two values?
column2: $\text{true}$ (often represented as 1) or $\text{false}$ (represented as 0).

column1: In terms of memory addressing, why does a $\text{bool}$ occupy 1 byte (8 bits) even though it only needs 1 bit of information?
column2: The byte is the smallest addressable unit of memory in a computer.

column1: Which C++ operator is used to determine the exact memory size of a data type or variable?
column2: The $\text{sizeof()}$ operator.

column1: What happens to a local variable in C++ if it is declared but not initialized with a value?
column2: It contains a "garbage value," which is unpredictable data from memory.

column1: What is the difference between a variable "declaration" and a variable "definition"?
column2: Declaration introduces the name/type, while definition allocates memory and optionally assigns an initial value.

column1: Which keyword is used to accept user input from the keyboard in C++?
column2: $\text{cin}$

column1: In the statement $\text{cin >> marks;}$, what is the symbol $\text{>>}$ called?
column2: The extraction operator.

column1: In the statement $\text{cout << "Hello";}$, what is the symbol $\text{<<}$ called?
column2: The insertion operator.

column1: How does an $\text{unsigned int}$ differ from a standard $\text{signed int}$?
column2: An $\text{unsigned int}$ only stores zero and positive values, allowing for a larger positive range.

column1: What is the mathematical formula for the maximum value an n-bit unsigned integer can hold?
The range of a signed n-bit integer is from $-2^{n-1}$ to _____.
What is the syntax for a single-line comment in C++?
column2: Two forward slashes ( // ).

column1: Under what condition will the code block inside an $\text{if}$ statement execute?
column2: It executes only if the provided condition evaluates to $\text{true}$.

column1: When using an $\text{if-else}$ structure, when does the $\text{else}$ block execute?
column2: It executes only when the preceding $\text{if}$ condition evaluates to $\text{false}$.

column1: In an $\text{if-else if-else}$ chain, what happens after the first $\text{true}$ condition is found and its block is executed?
column2: The rest of the conditions in the chain are skipped, and the program exits the structure.

column1: What is the term for placing one $\text{if}$ statement inside the body of another $\text{if}$ statement?
column2: Nested If.

column1: What is the primary advantage of a $\text{switch}$ statement over multiple $\text{if-else}$ conditions for discrete values?
column2: It is generally more readable and organized when comparing a single variable against many constants.

column1: In a $\text{switch}$ statement, what is the role of the $\text{break}$ keyword?
column2: It exits the switch block, preventing execution from continuing into subsequent cases.

column1: What occurs in a $\text{switch}$ statement if a $\text{case}$ matches but the $\text{break}$ keyword is missing?
column2: Fall-through behavior occurs, executing all following cases until a break or the end of the switch is reached.

column1: Which keyword in a $\text{switch}$ statement handles values that do not match any specified $\text{case}$?
column2: $\text{default}$

column1: List two data types that are NOT allowed in the expression of a $\text{switch}$ statement in C++.
column2: $\text{float}$ and $\text{string}$.

column1: What is the syntax for the Ternary Operator in C++?
column2: $\text{condition ? expression_if_true : expression_if_false;}$

column1: In C++, which control flow operator is often used as a shorthand replacement for simple $\text{if-else}$ statements?
column2: The Ternary Operator ( ? : ).

column1: Why would a developer use a loop in C++ instead of writing the same line of code multiple times?
column2: To handle repetitive tasks efficiently and reduce code redundancy.

column1: What are the three essential components defined within the parentheses of a standard $\text{for}$ loop?
column2: Initialization, Condition, and Updation.

column1: In the $\text{for}$ loop execution flow, when does the 'Initialization' step occur?
column2: It happens only once at the very beginning when the loop is first encountered.

column1: In a $\text{for}$ loop, what is the immediate next step after the loop's body code finishes executing?
column2: The Updation step (e.g., incrementing the loop counter).

column1: What happens if the 'Condition' in a $\text{for}$ loop evaluates to $\text{false}$ on the very first check?
column2: The loop body never executes, and the program moves to the next line after the loop.

column1: What is the function of the $\text{break}$ keyword when used inside a loop?
column2: It immediately terminates the loop and transfers control to the statement following the loop.

column1: What is the function of the $\text{continue}$ keyword when used inside a loop?
column2: It skips the remainder of the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration (starting with the update step).

column1: If a $\text{for}$ loop is written as $\text{for( ; ; )}$, what is the result?
column2: It creates an infinite loop because there is no condition to terminate it.

column1: What does one complete cycle of a loop's execution called?
column2: An iteration.

column1: How can you print a message that includes a variable value using $\text{cout}$?
column2: By chaining insertion operators, for example: $\text{cout << "Age: " << age << endl;}$

column1: In the context of the tutorial, why is C++ widely used in Game Development?
column2: Due to its ability to provide low-level memory management and high performance.

column1: What is the primary difference between $\text{float}$ and $\text{double}$ precision?
column2: $\text{double}$ can represent much larger numbers and more decimal places accurately than $\text{float}$.

column1: Which operator is used to check if two values are exactly equal in an $\text{if}$ condition?
column2: The equality operator ( == ).

column1: In C++, how do you represent the boolean value for "true" using an integer?
column2: Any non-zero value (typically 1).

column1: When using $\text{cin}$ to take a boolean input, what should the user type into the console for 'true'?
column2: The integer 1.

column1: What is the purpose of the 'Preprocessing' stage in C++ compilation?
column2: It processes directives starting with #, like including header files and expanding macros.

column1: What does the expression $\text{i = i + 1}$ do inside a loop?
column2: It increments the value of the variable $\text{i}$ by one in each iteration.

column1: Code Snippet: $\text{if(a > b) \{ cout << a; \} else \{ cout << b; \}}$
column2: This code prints the maximum of two numbers, $\text{a}$ and $\text{b}$.

column1: Why is it important to use different scopes (curly braces) for variables with the same name?
column2: To avoid "redefinition" errors by isolating the variables to specific blocks of code.
